steps.title: Identify the grammatical trigger
steps.content: The phrase "more likely" is an adjective expressing likelihood. Adjectives of likelihood (likely, certain, prone, apt) require an infinitive to follow.

steps.title: Apply the infinitive rule after likelihood adjectives
steps.content:
Rule: "likely" takes an infinitive complement ("likely + to + base verb").
  • Choice A — eaten: past participle — cannot follow "likely" directly; produces "were more likely eaten", which means they were probably consumed, not the intended meaning. ✗
  • Choice B — ate: simple past — a finite verb cannot serve as the complement after "likely"; produces an ungrammatical construction. ✗
  • Choice C — to eat: infinitive — correctly follows "more likely" to form "more likely to eat". ✓
  • Choice D — will be eating: future progressive — a finite verb phrase cannot follow "likely" as its complement. ✗
That is the correct answer.
